questions.question: Why is a crawler frontier usually partitioned by host or host hash instead of only by discovery time?
options: Because host-aware partitioning makes politeness and fairness enforceable.
Because discovery time is never stored for URLs.
Because partitioning by host guarantees duplicate content cannot occur.
Because search ranking depends only on host-level ordering.
questions.explanation: Per-host scheduling is central to safe crawling.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a lease on frontier work items?
options: It reduces HTML size before parsing.
It lets workers keep URLs forever until they choose to release them.
It allows recovery when a fetcher dies after claiming work.
It guarantees each URL is fetched exactly once globally.
questions.explanation: Leases make work reclaimable after failure.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that one domain is crowding out the rest of the crawl?
options: Average HTML title length across all pages.
Frontier age spread and queued URL share by host.
The number of parser binaries deployed this week.
Median redirect chain length for successful fetches.
questions.explanation: Skew shows up as host concentration and aging queues.

questions.question: Why should URL dedup and content dedup usually be separate stages?
options: Because URLs and fetched content can differ through redirects, canonicals, and near-duplicate pages.
Because content dedup only works for images, not HTML.
Because URL dedup requires stronger consistency than any search engine can provide.
Because running both stages violates robots.txt.
questions.explanation: They address different kinds of duplication.

questions.question: What is the trade-off in giving news domains a tighter recrawl target than the long tail?
options: You improve freshness for valuable content but spend more crawl budget unevenly.
You eliminate the need for content parsing.
You guarantee ranking quality without measuring change rates.
You make robots.txt caching unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Freshness tiers are a budget-allocation choice.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly mitigated by expiring frontier leases?
options: A host returning stale ETags.
A parser discovering too many duplicate links.
A fetcher crashing after claiming a batch of URLs.
A ranking model lowering a page score.

questions.question: Why is robots policy versioning useful in a distributed crawler?
options: It helps detect when workers are applying stale crawl policy decisions.
It compresses HTML response bodies in transit.
It removes the need for retry backoff after 5xx responses.
It makes near-duplicate content impossible.
questions.explanation: Versioning lets you audit policy drift.

questions.question: Which design change most improves product freshness without blindly raising fetch throughput everywhere?
options: Uniformly doubling fetchers for all domains.
Adding recrawl tiers based on change rate and page importance.
Removing host politeness delays during peak hours.
Persisting only discovered links and skipping page content.
questions.explanation: Targeted freshness policy is usually better than brute-force crawling.
